title: Gaeng Hang Lay (Northern Thai Pork Curry)

overview.description: A rich, slow-cooked Northern Thai curry influenced by Burmese cuisine, with tender pork in a fragrant sweet, sour and gently spiced sauce.

ingredients: 800g pork shoulder, cut into large chunks
method: Coat the pork with the Hang Lay curry paste.
Leave to marinate for at least 1 hour, preferably overnight.
Heat the oil over a medium-high heat.
Cook the onion for 8 minutes until softened.
Add the garlic and ginger and cook for 1 minute.
Return the pork to the pan.
Stir in the tamarind paste, palm sugar and fish sauce.
Add the pickled garlic, if using.
Cover and cook for 2½–3 hours until the pork is fork tender.
Remove the lid for the final 20 minutes if the sauce needs reducing.
Taste and adjust the balance of sweet, sour and salty before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Gaeng Hang Lay reflects the historical influence of neighbouring Myanmar on Northern Thai cuisine and is unusual among Thai curries because it contains no coconut milk.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build a slow-cooked curry, balance sweet, sour and savoury flavours, and cook pork shoulder until meltingly tender.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A completely different style of Thai curry that showcases the diversity of Thai regional cooking beyond coconut-based curries.
chapman-tips: Pork shoulder becomes wonderfully tender after a long, slow cook.
Marinating overnight develops a much deeper flavour.
The finished curry should taste balanced between sweet, sour and savoury rather than being dominated by any one flavour.
This is even better the next day.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze in family-sized portions for up to 6 months.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently on the hob; the flavour improves overnight.
